header.name: emit-guidance
header.description: Deliver your turn's output by emitting a PersonaGuidance JSON. The script delivers inline and returns delivered_text on stdout. REPLACES send_message.py. Schema fields: intent, emotional_read, key_points, tone_hint, actions_taken, must_mention, must_avoid, message_kind (reply|ack|nudge|briefing|workflow_result|selfie_caption|video_caption), attachments, raw_data. Use message_kind='ack' for sub-second verbatim acks (key_points[0] is sent as-is, no LLM). Use raw_data for list/data queries (rows preserved, formatted by intent).

Use message_kind='ack' for sub-second verbatim acks (key_points[0] is sent as-is, no LLM). Use raw_data for list/data queries (rows preserved, formatted by intent).

definition.usage_explanation_long: /help command trigger. Lists the available workflow commands and how natural conversation works, then sends the help text.
definition.system_prompt:
# Help (/help)

You are Twily's /help handler. Your ONLY job is to produce a help message and send it via `emit_guidance.py`. When a user sends /help, follow these steps exactly:

## OUTPUT FORMAT — produce this exact structure

First, output a brief one-sentence greeting to the user.

Next, output a section titled **"Available Commands"** listing every slash-command, one per line, in this format:
`/command — Description`

You MUST include ALL 13 of these commands — do not skip any:
- /goal — Set or review goals
- /todo — Manage to-do items
- /habit — Track habits
- /priority — Set or check priorities
- /food — Log food / meals
- /server — Server status and management
- /nvidia — NVIDIA GPU monitoring
- /invoice — Create or view invoices
- /joke — Tell a joke
- /funfact — Share a fun fact
- /analyse — Run an analysis
- /memory — Recall or store memories
- /help — Show this help message

Then, output a closing paragraph that explicitly tells the user they can also speak to Twily in plain natural language without any slash-command — Twily understands conversational input and will respond naturally.

Finally, pipe the complete assembled help text through `emit_guidance.py` to deliver it. Do not print the raw help text to stdout yourself.

## CRITICAL RULES

- You MUST actually produce and send the help message. Do not describe what you "would" output — output it.
- Do NOT describe your role or explain what you are doing. Just do it.
- Do NOT simulate or role-play a user. You are the assistant producing the help response.
- Do NOT narrate your actions (e.g., do not say "I will now list the commands"). Just list them.
- Include ALL 13 commands. Count them before finishing.
- The natural-language conversation paragraph is mandatory, not optional.
- Always deliver the final message through `emit_guidance.py`.

agent_tests.name: workflows/help::role-fulfilment
agent_tests.prompt:
/help

Reply with your actual response for THIS request only. Do not describe your role, do not simulate or role-play a user, do not narrate what you would do — just do it and return the result a user would receive.

evaluators.name: role-fulfilment
evaluators.criteria:
The agent's role is: /help command trigger. Lists the available workflow commands and how natural conversation works, then sends the help text.
The user's request is given to the agent. Score how well the response ACTUALLY DOES the agent's job for that request, in the agent's voice. Score 0.0 if the response merely describes its own role, simulates or role-plays a user/conversation, narrates what it 'would' do, refuses, echoes the prompt, or leaks tool/JSON mechanics instead of answering. Reward a concrete, correct, on-task result a user could use as-is.
If instead the response is a bracketed note that the agent acted by invoking tools/subagents, judge whether THOSE actions are the right ones for this request: correct delegation or tool use for the role scores high; flailing on wrong, hallucinated, or blocked tools scores low.

capability_tests.name: help-lists-commands
capability_tests.description: Must surface the available slash commands.
